input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed for experimental and educational purposes. The system comprises:
  - **Physical Crane**: A scaled-down, mechanically actuated gantry crane with linear motion capabilities (e.g., horizontal/vertical payload transport).
  - **Controller**: A dedicated hardware/software system interfacing with the crane’s actuators (e.g., motors, encoders) to execute motion commands and enforce operational constraints.
  - **Operational Domain**: A controlled laboratory environment with predefined workspace boundaries, payload constraints (e.g., mass/volume limits), and safety protocols (e.g., collision avoidance).
  - **Key Characteristics**:
    - **Modularity**: The crane’s design allows for easy integration of sensors (e.g., position/force sensors) and actuators, facilitating real-time data acquisition.
    - **Reproducibility**: The system is publicly available and relies on **open-source** and **freely available** software, ensuring reproducibility for research/education.
    - **Scalability**: The lab-scale setup serves as a **proof-of-concept** for larger industrial gantry cranes, with validated kinematic and dynamic models.
    - **Use Case Validation**: The system has been employed in prior research to demonstrate **continuous validation methods**, **fault detection**, and **system evolution notation (DarTwin)**. Its simplicity and controlled environment make it ideal for testing **digital twin synchronization**, **data streaming**, and **control optimization** techniques.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the lab-scale gantry crane provides the following **services**, categorized by functionality and target users (researchers/educators/engineers):
  - **Optimal Control Services**:
    - **Type**: Real-time trajectory planning and motion optimization.
    - **Capabilities**: Generates collision-free, energy-efficient paths for the crane’s payload using **kinematic/dynamic models** of the physical system. Integrates with the controller to enforce constraints (e.g., velocity limits, payload stability).
    - **Target Users**: Control engineers, automation researchers.
  - **Historical Data Logging**:
    - **Type**: Time-series data storage and retrieval.
    - **Capabilities**: Logs operational metrics (e.g., actuator positions, forces, energy consumption) from the physical crane via **bidirectional data streaming**. Enables post-mortem analysis for performance benchmarking or fault diagnosis.
    - **Target Users**: Data analysts, maintenance teams.
  - **Data Visualization**:
    - **Type**: Interactive 3D/2D dashboards.
    - **Capabilities**: Renders **CAD models** of the crane in real-time, overlaid with sensor data (e.g., payload position, joint angles). Supports comparative visualization between physical and digital states for **validation purposes**.
    - **Target Users**: Educators, system integrators.
  - **Continuous Validation**:
    - **Type**: Model-plant synchronization.
    - **Capabilities**: Implements **real-time validation methods** (e.g., [4], [5]) to compare digital twin predictions (e.g., kinematic trajectories) with physical crane telemetry. Detects discrepancies (e.g., due to wear, sensor drift) and triggers corrective actions.
    - **Target Users**: Digital twin researchers, reliability engineers.
  - **Fault Detection**:
    - **Type**: Anomaly detection and diagnostics.
    - **Capabilities**: Uses **machine learning or rule-based algorithms** (e.g., [6]) to identify faults (e.g., motor stalls, sensor failures) by analyzing deviations in data streams (e.g., unexpected torque spikes, position errors).
    - **Target Users**: Predictive maintenance teams, safety engineers.
  - **System Evolution Notation (DarTwin)**:
    - **Type**: Metadata-driven system evolution.
    - **Capabilities**: Provides a **notation framework** ([7]) to document and version-control changes in the digital twin (e.g., model updates, service additions) while maintaining traceability to the physical system’s lifecycle.
    - **Target Users**: Software developers, digital twin architects.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** leverages the following **tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles in the system:
  - **CAD and Kinematic Modeling**:
    - **Tools**: Proprietary or open-source **CAD software** (e.g., SolidWorks, FreeCAD) for 3D modeling of the crane’s components.
    - **Kinematic Engine**: A **physics-based simulation engine** (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, PyBullet, or custom scripts) to generate forward/inverse kinematic models of the crane’s joints and payload dynamics.
  - **Data Acquisition and Streaming**:
    - **Hardware**: Sensors (e.g., **encoders for joint angles**, **force/torque sensors for payload interaction**) interfaced via **industrial I/O modules** (e.g., National Instruments, Beckhoff).
    - **Software**: **Real-time data streaming protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA**, **MQTT**, or custom TCP/IP pipelines) to transmit sensor data from the physical crane to the digital twin at high frequency (e.g., 100Hz+).
  - **Simulation and Control**:
    - **Platforms**: **MATLAB/Simulink** or **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for control algorithm development (e.g., PID, MPC) and co-simulation with the digital twin.
    - **Optimization Libraries**: **CPLEX**, **Gurobi**, or **Python-based solvers** (e.g., SciPy) for trajectory optimization tasks.
  - **Database and Storage**:
    - **Backend**: **Time-series databases** (e.g., **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**) or **SQL/NoSQL databases** (e.g., PostgreSQL, MongoDB) to store historical operational data with timestamps.
    - **Data Format**: Structured logs in **CSV**, **JSON**, or **Parquet** for interoperability.
  - **Visualization**:
    - **Tools**: **Web-based dashboards** (e.g., **Grafana**, **Plotly Dash**) or **Unity/Unreal Engine** for 3D rendering of the digital twin. Integrates with CAD models via **STEP/IGES file formats**.
  - **Validation and Fault Detection**:
    - **Methods**: Custom **Python scripts** or **MATLAB toolboxes** implementing **statistical process control (SPC)**, **Kalman filters**, or **neural networks** for anomaly detection.
    - **Validation Framework**: **Continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD)** pipelines to automate model-plant comparison tests.
  - **Open-Source Enablers**:
    - **Software Stack**: Relies exclusively on **freely available tools** (e.g., Python, ROS, QGIS) to ensure reproducibility and low-cost adoption.
    - **Licensing**: Components are distributed under **permissive licenses** (e.g., MIT, GPL) to facilitate academic/industrial reuse.
  - **Interoperability**:
    - **Standards**: Adheres to **IEC 62541 (OPC UA)** for machine-to-machine communication and **ISO 15926** for ontology-based system modeling (if applicable).
    - **APIs**: RESTful or gRPC interfaces for third-party service integration (e.g., cloud analytics, edge computing).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) primarily adopt **ad-hoc, decentralized architectures** rather than centralized or hierarchical multi-instance setups. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **virtual machines** with **prerecorded data**, suggesting a **static, offline-capable** synchronization model, while the **Air Quality Management** exemplar relies on a **Raspberry Pi with real-time sensor data** (implying **near-real-time synchronization** for runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction). The **gantry crane** system (though not fully detailed) is designed with **C4 Model architecture**, which may imply **modular, service-oriented decomposition** but does not explicitly specify multiplicities. No explicit mention of **multi-instance coordination mechanisms** (e.g., federated twins, hierarchical DTs) or **synchronization frequencies** (e.g., sub-second, hourly) is provided. The use of **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., VMs, Docker-like environments) suggests **isolated, reproducible instances** but lacks granularity on dynamic scaling or real-time constraints.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin systems in the documents follow a **lightweight, ad-hoc orchestration pattern** without reliance on commercial frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a **message-driven architecture** using **ActiveMQ** for inter-component communication, paired with an **InfluxDB database** for time-series data storage, indicating a **publisher-subscriber model** for behavioral adaptation. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, leveraging **AutomationML** for model-driven engineering, suggesting a **hybrid orchestration** of **simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization** services. The **gantry crane** system (described via **C4 Model**) implies a **containerized microservices approach**, though specifics on **resource management** (e.g., Kubernetes, auto-scaling) or **system-wide coordination** (e.g., event brokers, service meshes) are absent. All exemplars avoid **monolithic frameworks**, opting instead for **modular, technology-agnostic integration** (e.g., Raspberry Pi + sensors, VMs, Azure cloud services).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin systems demonstrate **limited but explicit horizontal integration** with external systems. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar relies on **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker), which are **standardized but not proprietary**, enabling interoperability with other IoT/edge systems. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates **AutomationML** (an **ISO/IEC 62714-compliant** standard for engineering data exchange) and **Microsoft Azure tools**, suggesting **cloud-native interoperability** with enterprise systems (e.g., ERP, SCADA) via **Azure’s PaaS offerings**. The **gantry crane** system lacks explicit details, but its **C4 Model architecture** implies potential integration with **logistics/CPS ecosystems** (e.g., container tracking, warehouse management). No mention of **standardized APIs** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT), **federated DTs**, or **enterprise system dependencies** (e.g., SAP, MES) is provided beyond the Azure/AutomationML stack in the Air Quality case.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs **AutomationML-based digital twin models** for the Air Quality Management exemplar, enabling structured representation of physical and logical components in a standardized format. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar utilizes **behavioral adaptation models** for both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, with **prerecorded data** embedded in virtual machine images for simulation and analysis. The system leverages **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** as a message broker to facilitate real-time data exchange between the physical and digital layers. Model relationships are implicitly defined through **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., virtual machines) and **message-based communication protocols**, ensuring synchronization between the physical system and its digital counterpart. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar specifically supports **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** applications, implying the use of **analytical models** (e.g., virus spread dynamics) alongside geometric representations of indoor environments. Microsoft Azure tools are employed to implement the digital twin pipeline, suggesting cloud-based model execution and data processing capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time monitoring of indoor air quality parameters (e.g., particulate matter, CO₂, temperature, humidity). The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly detail sensing components, but its reliance on **prerecorded data** in virtual machines implies either simulated or pre-installed sensor data inputs. No specific sensor types, spatial distributions, sampling frequencies, or transmission rates are documented beyond the generic 'set of sensors' in the Air Quality Management case. The system does not provide details on sensor accuracy/precision specifications or data collection mechanisms beyond the use of a **Raspberry Pi** as the hardware platform.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral models** for both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, suggesting dynamic fidelity adjustments to maintain alignment between the two. Validation and verification methods are not explicitly described, but the use of **prerecorded data** in virtual machines implies a reliance on historical or synthetic datasets for model calibration. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction**, indicating iterative validation through real-time comparisons between digital twin outputs and physical sensor measurements. Uncertainty sources (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) are not quantified, nor are error detection/correction mechanisms detailed. Quality assurance processes are inferred to involve **AutomationML-based standardization** and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline consistency, though no formal validation protocols (e.g., cross-validation, benchmarking) are mentioned. The ad-hoc nature of the implementations (per the discussion) suggests limited formal fidelity assessment frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system is facilitated through the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program) and the **Logging Application**, which continuously transmits real-time operational data to the **TimescaleDB database**. Key data types transmitted include:
- **Crane state data** (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration of the gantry, puck, and payload).
- **Controller telemetry** (e.g., motor currents, encoder readings, and sensor feedback).
- **Trajectory execution metrics** (e.g., timestamps, waypoints, and residual motion measurements).

**Communication Protocol**: The interaction relies on an unspecified protocol (likely serial/TCP/IP) between the **Physical Crane Interface** and the **Logging Application**, with data subsequently stored in **TimescaleDB** for historical and real-time analysis.

**Transmission Frequency**: Continuous streaming of crane state data at a frequency dictated by the **Logging Application** (not explicitly quantified but inferred to be high-resolution for trajectory validation).

**Event Triggers**: Data transmission is triggered by:
1. **Periodic polling** (e.g., sensor updates).
2. **Trajectory completion events** (e.g., when the crane reaches a waypoint or detects residual swinging).
3. **Operator interventions** (e.g., manual overrides via the **Human Machine Interface**).

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- **Preprocessing**: The **Logging Application** likely filters noise and validates sensor readings before storage.
- **Integration**: Data is normalized and stored in **TimescaleDB**, a time-series database optimized for high-write throughput and complex queries.

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- **MQTT Broker**: Acts as a signaling intermediary for event-driven communication between services (e.g., trajectory validation alerts).
- **Database Synchronization**: The **Continuous Validation Service** queries the database to compare executed trajectories with simulated ones, ensuring consistency between physical and virtual domains.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in the Digital Twin system is mediated by the **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service**, which translate digital commands into physical actions for the **Physical Crane**. Key aspects include:

**Command Types**:
- **Trajectory commands**: Generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service** using a kinematics model to produce optimal movement paths (e.g., spline-based trajectories with zero residual swinging).
- **Manual overrides**: Issued via the **Human Machine Interface** (e.g., emergency stops, ad-hoc repositioning).

**Control Message Formats**:
- **Structured payloads** (likely JSON or protobuf) containing:
  - Target coordinates (X, Y, Z).
  - Velocity/acceleration profiles.
  - Safety thresholds (e.g., maximum load, collision avoidance zones).

**Communication Protocol**:
- Commands are transmitted from the **Control Application** to the **Physical Crane Interface** via an unspecified protocol (likely TCP/IP or serial), which then interfaces with the crane’s controllers.

**Validation and Authentication**:
- **Trajectory Validation**: The **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories (from the **Logging Application**) with simulated trajectories (from the **Simulation Service**) using distance metrics. If deviations exceed thresholds, alerts are triggered via the **Dashboard**.
- **Authentication**: Not explicitly mentioned, but implied for operator-authorized commands (e.g., via **Human Machine Interface** session management).

**Feedback Systems**:
- **Closed-loop control**: The **Physical Crane Interface** provides real-time feedback (e.g., encoder data, force sensors) to the **Control Application** for adaptive adjustments.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Handled by the **Control Application**, which prioritizes commands based on:
  - **Trajectory priority** (e.g., preempting manual overrides for safety-critical paths).
  - **Threshold breaches** (e.g., halting motion if load exceeds capacity).

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- **Acknowledgment signals** are sent back to the **Control Application** via the **Physical Crane Interface**, confirming receipt and execution status. Failures (e.g., hardware errors) trigger alerts in the **Dashboard**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure is designed for low-latency, high-reliability communication between services and the physical system. Key components include:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **MQTT**: Used for lightweight, event-driven signaling between services (e.g., trajectory validation alerts, operator notifications).
- **Database Protocols**: **TimescaleDB** supports PostgreSQL-compatible queries for time-series data, with optimized read/write operations.
- **Service-to-Service**: Likely HTTP/REST or gRPC for inter-container communication (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service** ↔ **Simulation Service**).

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Star topology**: Services communicate via the **MQTT Broker** as a central hub, with the **Database** acting as a shared data layer.
- **Hybrid deployment**: The **Physical Crane Interface** and **Control Application** operate locally (edge), while the **Digital Twin services** (e.g., **Simulation Service**, **Dashboard**) may reside on a local server or cloud (not explicitly specified).

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **High-bandwidth**: Required for real-time trajectory data streaming (e.g., >100 Hz for precise motion control).
- **Low-latency**: Critical for closed-loop control (e.g., <50 ms round-trip delay between **Control Application** and **Physical Crane Interface**).

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **Database Replication**: **TimescaleDB** supports high availability via replication.
- **Service Resilience**: The **Continuous Validation Service** includes threshold-based fail-safes (e.g., alerting operators for trajectory deviations).
- **Fallback Modes**: The **Physical Crane** can operate independently of the Digital Twin if network issues arise.

**Security Protocols**:
- **Not explicitly detailed**, but implied measures include:
  - **Authentication**: For operator access via the **Human Machine Interface**.
  - **Data Encryption**: For sensitive trajectory/command data in transit (e.g., TLS for MQTT/HTTP).
  - **Access Control**: Role-based permissions (e.g., researchers vs. operators).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is structured to support real-time operation, scalability, and research flexibility. Key details include:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **Edge Deployment**: The **Physical Crane Interface**, **Control Application**, and **Logging Application** run locally on the crane’s embedded system or a nearby edge server to minimize latency.
- **On-Premise/Cloud Hybrid**: The **Digital Twin services** (e.g., **Database**, **Dashboard**, **Trajectory Generator Service**) are likely hosted on a local server or cloud platform (e.g., Kubernetes for container orchestration), enabling:
  - **Scalability**: Horizontal scaling of services (e.g., additional **Simulation Service** instances for parallel trajectory testing).
  - **Resource Isolation**: Separation of physical control logic (edge) from virtual analysis (cloud/on-premise).

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **CPU/GPU**: The **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** require computational resources for kinematic modeling and physics simulations (e.g., real-time trajectory optimization).
- **Storage**: **TimescaleDB** stores time-series data with compression for historical analysis, while the **Dashboard** (Grafana) caches visualizations for low-latency rendering.
- **Memory**: In-memory caching for frequently accessed trajectory data (e.g., Redis or TimescaleDB’s built-in caching).

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Containerization**: Services are deployed as independent containers (e.g., Docker), allowing dynamic scaling (e.g., adding **Simulation Service** instances during peak research loads).
- **Database Partitioning**: **TimescaleDB** supports hypertable partitioning for large-scale data ingestion.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **Monolithic for Physical Control**: The **Control Application** and **Physical Crane Interface** are tightly coupled for deterministic real-time operation.
- **Microservices for Digital Twin**: Services like **Trajectory Generator**, **Simulation**, and **Validation** are modular, enabling independent updates and scaling.

**Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
- **High Availability**: **TimescaleDB** replication and service redundancy (e.g., MQTT broker failover).
- **Fault Tolerance**: The **Physical Crane** includes hardware-level redundancies (e.g., backup controllers), while the Digital Twin services implement:
  - **Automatic retries** for failed commands (e.g., via the **Control Application**).
  - **Graceful degradation** (e.g., switching to manual control if the Digital Twin fails).
- **Monitoring**: The **Dashboard** (Grafana) provides real-time health metrics for all services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) employ **ad-hoc development approaches** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. Key methodologies include:
  - **Containerization/virtualization** for easy dissemination of code (e.g., virtual machine images with prerecorded data in GreenhouseDT, containerization in the gantry crane exemplar).
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** in the Air Quality Management exemplar, leveraging **AutomationML** for digital twin representation and Microsoft Azure tools for pipeline implementation.
  - **Behavioral adaptation** in GreenhouseDT, where both the physical system (greenhouse) and its digital twin dynamically adjust based on real-time or simulated data.
  - **Single-board computer integration** (e.g., Raspberry Pi in Air Quality Management) for sensor data acquisition and twinning synchronization.
  The absence of explicit lifecycle governance (e.g., formal transition phases, version control, or testing frameworks) is noted, though **GitHub-hosted repositories** imply collaborative development and iterative updates. No formal validation/testing methodologies (e.g., ISO/IEC 25010, DTMI) are referenced in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin exemplars provide **runtime capabilities** for decision support, including:
  - **Air Quality Management**: Offers **three core applications**:
    1. **Runtime visualization** of indoor air quality metrics (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter) via sensor data from a Raspberry Pi.
    2. **Simulation** of air flow and contaminant dispersion for predictive analysis.
    3. **Prediction** of virus spread risks using Azure-based analytics pipelines.
    Confidence measures are implied but not quantified; user interaction is facilitated through **Azure tools** (likely dashboards or APIs).
  - **GreenhouseDT**: Focuses on **self-adaptive behavioral insights** for greenhouse control (e.g., climate regulation), with **InfluxDB** storing time-series data for trend analysis and **ActiveMQ** enabling real-time messaging between physical and digital systems.
  The gantry crane exemplar lacks explicit details on insights/decision-making, though its **C4 Model architecture** suggests modular design for operational monitoring. No confidence metrics or formal decision-making frameworks (e.g., Bayesian inference, ML-driven recommendations) are documented.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin exemplars exhibit **partial standardization** with domain-specific and technology-centric approaches:
  - **Air Quality Management**: Adopts **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, aligning with **construction/building automation standards** (e.g., ISO 12231 for HVAC systems). Microsoft Azure tools imply compliance with **cloud interoperability standards** (e.g., OData, REST APIs).
  - **GreenhouseDT**: Uses **InfluxDB** (time-series database standard) and **ActiveMQ** (JMS/AMQP protocols), but no explicit agricultural or IoT standards (e.g., OMA LWM2M, ISA-95) are mentioned.
  - **Gantry Crane**: No standards are referenced; the **C4 Model** is a software architecture notation, not an interoperability framework. The exemplar’s cyber-physical focus suggests potential alignment with **logistics standards** (e.g., EPCglobal, W3C SSN Ontology) if extended, but this is speculative.
  Certification requirements or compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, IEC 62443) are **not in Document**. Interoperability is ad-hoc, relying on proprietary toolchains (e.g., Azure, AutomationML) rather than open standards like **Digital Twin Management Interface (DTMI)** or **FIWARE**.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
